spec_match.rationale: Demonstrates the spec's headline use case: two visually-styled arenas where the right one is wrapped in `@supports at-rule(@scope)` so the enhancement only applies on browsers that parse both the conditional and the gated rule.

interactive.rationale: The two arenas render side-by-side and a JS verdict line reports browser support — but there are zero user controls (`CONTROLS_COUNT: 0`). The page is interactive only in the 'browser changes the rendering automatically' sense; there's no slider/toggle to flip the wrapper on/off and see the contrast manually.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'ship enhanced styling that only paints when the browser supports an at-rule — purely declarative, no JavaScript class toggles. Live before/after.' — page delivers that without JS class toggles.

edge_cases.rationale: Handles three cases (browser doesn't recognise at-rule() conditional, recognises but rule unsupported, fully supported) in the verdict line. Never lets the user simulate a non-supporting browser to see the fallback even when their own browser supports it — a 'force fallback' toggle would teach the spec better.

references.rationale: See-also lists https://chromestatus.com/feature/5110744177836032 and the css-conditional-5 spec.

design_system.rationale: Mostly CSS variables — but the page contains a raw hex `#f1faf4` for what looks like a soft emerald wash. Use a token (`var(--accent-emerald)` with reduced alpha via colour-mix, or a new --bg-emerald-wash token) so the wash tracks the design system.

openQuestions.title: Add a `force fallback` toggle
openQuestions.detail: Many visitors will be on Chrome 143+ and see only the enhanced arena. A 'simulate unsupported browser' switch that adds a parent class which selectively undoes the @supports-gated CSS would let everyone see the two states without needing two browsers.

openQuestions.title: Replace the `#f1faf4` raw hex
openQuestions.detail: Pull the soft emerald wash from a token. Either define `--bg-emerald-wash: color-mix(in oklch, var(--accent-emerald), white 70%)` in public/styles.css and use it, or use the existing accent directly. CLAUDE.md rule #5 says no raw hex.

openQuestions.title: Gate on something more contemporary than @scope
openQuestions.detail: @scope has been in Chrome since 118, so almost every visitor will see the enhanced arena and miss the fallback. Pick a fresher conditional (`@starting-style` is still spotty, `@position-try` is Chrome-only) to make the demo's purpose more visible.

openQuestions.title: Show the CSS source side-by-side with the rendered arenas
openQuestions.detail: The page renders the two arenas and a verdict but never shows the CSS that's gating them. A code panel with the `@supports at-rule(@scope) { … }` wrapper highlighted would close the gap between visual outcome and the actual spec syntax.

summary: Strong declarative demo of the spec's main use case — but interactivity is thin (no controls), and the @scope gate is now too widely supported to teach the fallback path. A 'force fallback' toggle would fix both at once.
